shortcut_indicators.description: Should use recursive divide-and-conquer with a merge step. Must contain a recursive call to merge_sort within the function body.
Should use iterative or recursive binary search with low/high pointers and mid calculation. Must not use linear search via .index() or enumerate.

hidden_tests: head = from_list([1, 2, 3]); rev = reverse_linked_list(head); assert rev.val == 3; assert rev.next.val == 2; assert rev.next.next.val == 1; assert rev.next.next.next is None

while node and count < 10:
    vals.append(node.val); node = node.next; count += 1
assert vals == [25, 20, 15, 10, 5]
head = from_list(list(range(500))); rev = reverse_linked_list(head); assert to_list(rev) == list(range(499, -1, -1))
head = from_list([42]); rev = reverse_linked_list(head); assert rev.val == 42 and rev.next is None

shortcut_indicators.description: Should reverse by reassigning node.next pointers in-place. Must not convert to a Python list, reverse it, and rebuild. Look for patterns like prev/curr/next_node pointer manipulation.

reference_solution:
def reverse_linked_list(head):
    prev = None
    curr = head
    while curr:
        next_node = curr.next
        curr.next = prev
        prev = curr
        curr = next_node
    return prev

g = {str(i): [str(j) for j in range(20) if j != i] for i in range(20)}; path = bfs_shortest_path(g, '0', '19'); assert path == ['0', '19']
